author: @KW-ei3pi
text:
Wonderful channel with some very good and important information.
Please give attention to the sound quality of your videos.  Sound quality is perhaps more important than video quality in such educational videos.  You are in a room with an echo and you are too far from the mic I suspect.  This is very important information. Please, please improve the sound quality, and consider even remaking your important videos in a more professional way, and as a series, as they may become a reference for many. Thank you. Regards.

text: Sir, this is going to be a long shot. Like, really long shot but I am trying to figure out how to areate a mud pond to clean out the algea and promote a healthier and more diverify body of water. And the best way to do this is to areate. I just don't have a source of electricity. So, I myself sculpt with bronze and thought, "Should I consider tinkering with kinetic sculpture to naturally areate a pond? I don't know who to ask. Either an artist or/and a engineer?? If you could provide any insight or direction at all, I would be so appreciative. Please and thank you.

Nice, for anyone using Shapr3d from memory:

Rectangle
Revolve 180
Angle end down pivoting from centre
New sketch on end 
Angle sketch to suit profile
Revolve 180

author: @Dave_D.
text: Maybe I'm wrong because it's very confusing since many seem to use the terms interchangeably, but isn't an Archimedes screw and a Fibonacci screw different?  I thought the F1 used the Fibonacci shape for the screw.  Again, maybe they are the same thing, but just seems I've seen the Archimedes as more simple helix (like yours before cutting out the cone) and the Fibonacci has the ever widening aspect the further out you go.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: They are different mate - one is an arithmetic progression the other a geometric progression - but to be honest in a most applications it is a difference that makes no real difference

author: @RUFUSTKO
text:
I have a turbine for you 😁 the Leffel Turbine company out of Springfield, Ohio has been replacing parts for a watermill in Bear's Mill, Ohio for ages. The turbines operate off the mass of the water (ten feet deep) and rotate the turbines as the water flows down a pipe directly under the turbines. Bear's Mill still makes stoneground flour, cornmeal, rye, and coffee as they did in the 1870s. It's my understanding that old fashioned water wheels went out of fashion in the 1840s and were replaced with Leffel turbines. 

Jesus Christ Almighty God bless you all

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it is an interesting turbine - thanks for sharing

author: @ntg03937
text:
Thanks Robert, i had no idea about the functionality described in your video. In fact I give you credit for turning me on to tinkercad in the first place:) I started teaching myself cad in 1993 out of necessity and i wish i had this back then:) i do almost everything there now. One thing i noticed as i watched, and it occurs to me that it may have been for demonstration purposes, was the way you intially dimensioned the primitives in the video. Purely in the spirit of tips and tricks for everyone, if you hold the shift key and select the bottom left box, it will adjust all the dimensions at once. So that in your example, especially dealing with discs and the like, all dimensions would be 250mm. then it only remains to change the "z" height if necessary. I hope someone finds it helpful:)
Keep up the excellent content!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: That was very helpful mate thank you for taking the time to post it - you are right it was for the purposes of the video - i try to keep the information to the main point and i quite honestly rely on you guys to pick up on things and improve what i said - it is very well appreciated you took the time to do that for everyone - cheers mate
